name: 2023-07-basin
note: Basin (Beanstalk Wells AMM + MultiFlow pump oracle). Findings verified against the official C4 report (1 High / 13 Medium) and grounded to the clone's source lines. IDs carry the report's H-/M- number.

known_findings.summary: Well.sync() (and shift()) call _setReserves directly without first pushing the previous-block reserves into the attached pump (unlike swapFrom), so the pump's time-weighted reserves never capture the pre-change state; an attacker can move balances and sync/shift in the same block to manipulate the oracle reserves consumers read (H-01).

storeUint128's odd-reserve branch re-reads the target slot's high 128 bits via shl(128, shr(128, sload(...))) and re-packs them; the slot/bit arithmetic mis-preserves an adjacent packed reserve, corrupting stored well reserves and thus the pump's oracle values (M-02/M-03). Low-level packing logic, no modeled detector.

getBytes32FromBytes bounds-checks with `index > data.length` instead of `>=`, allowing an out-of-bounds memory read of the last word, which can return corrupted bytes and lead to loss of funds (M-01). Low-level memory-bounds logic, no modeled detector.

calcLpTokenSupply computes the LP supply as sqrt(reserves[0]*reserves[1]*EXP_PRECISION); the integer sqrt loses precision asymmetrically versus the division used elsewhere, so excess reserve tokens can be left in / leaked from the pool over many operations (M-06). Precision/rounding-direction.

The pump treats the immutable BLOCK_TIME as a permanent constant (blocksPassed = deltaTimestamp / BLOCK_TIME in _capReserve), so if average block time changes the per-block reserve-change cap is applied over the wrong number of blocks, biasing the time-weighted oracle (M-08/M-13). Block-time-as-elapsed-time assumption.
